subject: ap-government
title: Argument Essay — Federal Government vs. States in Ensuring Educational Opportunities
stimulus: The federal government and the states each play an important role in public education. Take a position on whether the federal government or the states are more effective in ensuring educational opportunities for all students.

parts.prompt: Respond to the prompt with a defensible claim or thesis that establishes a line of reasoning. (Use at least one piece of evidence from one of the following foundational documents: Fourteenth Amendment of the United States Constitution, Tenth Amendment of the United States Constitution, Article I of the United States Constitution.)
parts.scoring_notes: Must take a clear position. Generic statements about there being a debate do not earn the point.

scoring_points.description: Row A — Thesis/Claim: Respond to the prompt with a defensible claim or thesis that establishes a line of reasoning.

required_elements: Defensible claim establishing a line of reasoning
alternatives.correct_example: “The federal government is more effective at ensuring educational opportunities for all students because the federal government has more resources to address issues of inequality than do individual states.”

required_elements: Defensible claim establishing a line of reasoning
alternatives.correct_example: “The federal government is more effective at ensuring educational opportunities for all students because it can create national policies that apply to all the states.”

required_elements: Defensible claim establishing a line of reasoning
alternatives.correct_example: “State governments are more effective at ensuring educational opportunities for all students because states are closer to citizens and can develop solutions to fit the particular issues that affect the states.”

wrong_examples: “There is a debate about whether the federal or state governments should ensure educational equality.” Do not respond to the prompt
“There are times when states do things that help their citizens out better than the national government.”
common_traps: Restating the prompt without making a claim
Describing the issue without taking a position

parts.prompt: Support your claim with at least TWO pieces of specific and relevant evidence. One piece of evidence must come from one of the foundational documents listed. A second piece of evidence can come from any other foundational document or from course concepts.
parts.scoring_notes: To earn 2 or 3 points, must have earned Row A. To earn 3 points, one piece of evidence must come from the Fourteenth Amendment, Tenth Amendment, or Article I.

scoring_points.description: Row B — Evidence (0 points): No accurate evidence or evidence not relevant to the topic.

required_elements: No accurate or relevant evidence
alternatives.correct_example: No points earned — evidence is inaccurate or irrelevant to the prompt.

scoring_points.description: Row B — Evidence (1 point): Provides one piece of evidence relevant to the topic of the prompt.

required_elements: Provide one piece of evidence relevant to the topic of the prompt.
alternatives.correct_example: Provide one piece of evidence relevant to the topic of the prompt.

required_elements: May or may not have a claim or thesis.
alternatives.correct_example: May or may not have a claim or thesis.

common_traps: Evidence must be relevant but need not directly support a specific claim to earn 1 point

scoring_points.description: Row B — Evidence (2 points): Uses one piece of specific and relevant evidence to support the claim/thesis OR provides two pieces of relevant evidence.

required_elements: Provide one piece of specific and relevant evidence that supports the claim or thesis. This evidence can come from one of the foundational documents listed in the prompt, any other foundational document, or from knowledge of course concepts.
alternatives.correct_example: Provide one piece of specific and relevant evidence that supports the claim or thesis. This evidence can come from one of the foundational documents listed in the prompt, any other foundational document, or from knowledge of course concepts.

common_traps: To earn 2 points, the response must have earned Row A (claim/thesis)

scoring_points.description: Row B — Evidence (3 points): Uses two pieces of specific and relevant evidence to support the claim, one from a listed foundational document.

required_elements: Provide two pieces of specific and relevant evidence that support the claim or thesis. One of these pieces of evidence must come from a foundational document listed in the prompt. The other piece of evidence can come from a different foundational document or from knowledge of course concepts. Examples that do not earn points: Provide evidence that is not specific
alternatives.correct_example: Provide two pieces of specific and relevant evidence that support the claim or thesis. One of these pieces of evidence must come from a foundational document listed in the prompt. The other piece of evidence can come from a different foundational document or from knowledge of course concepts. Examples that do not earn points: Provide evidence that is not specific

required_elements: “Parts of the Constitution support the idea that the federal government is more important than state governments.” Provide evidence that is not relevant to the topic of the prompt
alternatives.correct_example: “Parts of the Constitution support the idea that the federal government is more important than state governments.” Provide evidence that is not relevant to the topic of the prompt

required_elements: “State governments share some powers with the federal government.” Examples of evidence that are relevant to the topic of the prompt:
alternatives.correct_example: “State governments share some powers with the federal government.” Examples of evidence that are relevant to the topic of the prompt:

required_elements: Equal protection clause
alternatives.correct_example: Equal protection clause

required_elements: Laboratories of democracy
alternatives.correct_example: Laboratories of democracy

required_elements: Reserved powers
alternatives.correct_example: Reserved powers

required_elements: Civil Rights Act
alternatives.correct_example: Civil Rights Act

required_elements: Title IX
alternatives.correct_example: Title IX

required_elements: Brown v. Board of Education Examples of acceptable specific and relevant evidence that support the claim or thesis (one example is one piece of evidence):
alternatives.correct_example: Brown v. Board of Education Examples of acceptable specific and relevant evidence that support the claim or thesis (one example is one piece of evidence):

required_elements: “The federal government can make national laws like Title IX of the Education Amendments Act to ensure that there is not discrimination on the basis of sex in educational policy.”
alternatives.correct_example: “The federal government can make national laws like Title IX of the Education Amendments Act to ensure that there is not discrimination on the basis of sex in educational policy.”

required_elements: “States are useful as testing grounds for various policies that might provide equal access to educational opportunities prior to the federal government passing a national law.” Examples of acceptable specific and relevant evidence from the foundational documents that support the claim or thesis (one example is one piece of evidence):
alternatives.correct_example: “States are useful as testing grounds for various policies that might provide equal access to educational opportunities prior to the federal government passing a national law.” Examples of acceptable specific and relevant evidence from the foundational documents that support the claim or thesis (one example is one piece of evidence):

required_elements: “The Federal government could use the equal protection clause of the Fourteenth Amendment to require equal treatment in educational policy.”
alternatives.correct_example: “The Federal government could use the equal protection clause of the Fourteenth Amendment to require equal treatment in educational policy.”

required_elements: “The reserved powers under the Tenth Amendment reserves the power to determine educational policy to the states.”
alternatives.correct_example: “The reserved powers under the Tenth Amendment reserves the power to determine educational policy to the states.”

required_elements: “Article I of the Constitution gives Congress the power to pass legislation that addresses education at the national level.”
alternatives.correct_example: “Article I of the Constitution gives Congress the power to pass legislation that addresses education at the national level.”

common_traps: One piece must be from a foundational document listed in the prompt

parts.prompt: Use reasoning to explain why your evidence supports your claim or thesis.
parts.scoring_notes: Must have earned Row A and at least 2 points in Row B. Must explain why the evidence supports the specific claim.

scoring_points.description: Row C — Reasoning: Use reasoning (classification, process, causation, or comparison) to explain how or why the evidence supports the claim or thesis.

required_elements: Reasoning that connects evidence to claim
alternatives.correct_example: “If a law passed by a state yields excellent results, that policy can be adopted by other states that believe it will also work for them, and it can also be adopted by the federal government.”

required_elements: Reasoning that connects evidence to claim
alternatives.correct_example: “The equal protection clause of the Fourteenth Amendment has been used as a basis for the federal government to pass laws such as the Civil Rights Act of 1964 which has been remarkably effective in addressing equality issues.”

common_traps: Must have earned Row A and at least 2 points in Row B
Simply restating evidence without reasoning does not earn this point

parts.prompt: Respond to an opposing or alternate perspective using refutation, concession, or rebuttal.
parts.scoring_notes: Must have earned Row A. Must describe an alternate perspective AND refute, concede, or rebut it.

scoring_points.description: Row D — Responds to Alternate Perspectives: Respond to an opposing or alternate perspective using rebuttal or refutation.

required_elements: Describes alternate perspective AND rebuts/refutes it
alternatives.correct_example: “Some might argue that the federal government is more effective because it has greater resources, but federal laws are ‘one size fits all’ and might not always address the specific needs of states and communities.”

required_elements: Describes alternate perspective AND rebuts/refutes it
alternatives.correct_example: “While some argue that states are better suited to ensure educational equality because they are better equipped to meet local needs, state governments also have a long history of discrimination against minorities through Jim Crow laws and other segregationist policies, which requires the federal government to establish uniform policy to ensure equality in education across the states.”

wrong_examples: “Some argue that states’ rights should take priority.” Describe an alternate perspective but do not refute, concede, or rebut that perspective
“Some people say that the federal government is better at ensuring educational equality but that is just flat out wrong.”
common_traps: Simply identifying an alternate perspective without rebutting/refuting it does not earn this point
